{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.95, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.4": 0.6, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.5}}

Summary: Identified a load-bearing contradiction (the section claims "training-free" / "no routing-specific supervision" while describing supervised training on RouterBench labels), flagged two further overclaims, and produced a revised draft that reframes the contribution honestly.

## The central problem

The framing collapses on itself. Paragraph 1 says EcoRoute "requires no routing-specific supervision" and is "immediately deployable in any new domain without a data-collection phase." Paragraph 3 then describes training a scoring head on 50,000 RouterBench triples with GPT-4-as-judge quality labels — which *is* routing-specific supervision, just borrowed from someone else's corpus. Paragraph 5 then re-asserts "the absence of any labeling burden" as the source of the gains, which is the same overclaim a third time.

You have two honest options:

1. **Keep the supervised head, drop the "training-free" framing.** Position EcoRoute as a *lightweight, transferable* router trained once on a public benchmark and deployed zero-shot to new domains. The novelty is then "trained on RouterBench, generalizes off-distribution" — which is a defensible empirical claim if you have the evidence.
2. **Actually make it training-free.** Replace the trained scoring head with something prompt-only — e.g., zero-shot embedding similarity to per-model exemplar prompts, or an LLM-as-router prefilter. Then the original framing holds, but the experimental section needs to change.

The draft below takes option (1), which matches what the method section actually describes.

## Other issues to address before revision

- **"Model-pool agnosticism" is wrong as stated.** The scoring head produces *per-model* utility scores, so its output dimensionality is tied to the training pool. Adding a new LLM requires extending the head and (probably) retraining. What you actually have is *constant routing latency regardless of pool size at inference*, which is a weaker but true claim.
- **The headline result mixes two framings.** "92% of GPT-4's quality at 18% of cost" and "7 quality points over cascade at matched cost" are different operating points. Pick one as headline, report both in the table, and state which axis is being held fixed.
- **Delta vs. RouteLLM is underspecified.** RouteLLM also trains on preference data and is the closest baseline; it should appear in the baseline list, not just the related-work sentence. Otherwise reviewers will ask why it was excluded.
- **Citations missing.** FrugalGPT, RouteLLM, RouterBench, and GPT-4-as-judge each need a reference on first mention.
- **"Utility-minus-cost score"** is introduced without saying how cost enters — is it a fixed scalar, a Lagrangian with a tunable λ, a per-token price? One clause fixes this.

## Revised draft

We present EcoRoute, a lightweight router that directs user queries across a heterogeneous pool of large language models. EcoRoute is trained once on a public preference corpus and deployed zero-shot to new domains: at deployment time, no in-domain routing labels are collected. This distinguishes it from per-deployment classifiers (FrugalGPT [cite]) and matches the transfer setting studied by RouteLLM [cite], against which we compare directly.

The router runs in three stages. An embedding model maps the prompt to a fixed-dimensional vector; a scoring head produces a per-model utility estimate; the model maximizing utility − λ·cost is selected, where λ is a user-set cost-quality tradeoff and cost is the provider's per-token price. Only the selected model is then queried.

The scoring head is trained on the RouterBench [cite] training split, in which each prompt is paired with responses from every candidate LLM and quality judgments from GPT-4-as-judge [cite]. We sample 50,000 (prompt, model_a, model_b) triples and minimize a pairwise logistic ranking loss. Training converges in roughly four GPU-hours on a single A100. At inference the head adds ~3 ms of CPU overhead per prompt, and because no candidate LLM is invoked until routing completes, this overhead is independent of pool size.

We evaluate against four baselines: uniform random, cost-only, the FrugalGPT cascade, and RouteLLM. On the RouterBench evaluation split, EcoRoute reaches 92% of GPT-4's average quality at 18% of its cost; at matched cost it exceeds the FrugalGPT cascade by 7 quality points and RouteLLM by [X] points. [If you also have an out-of-distribution generalization experiment — which is what the "no in-domain labels" framing demands — report it here; without it, the transfer claim is unsupported.]

## Uncertainty

The RouteLLM comparison number is a placeholder — I do not know your actual result against that baseline. The citation keys are placeholders; standard references exist for all four works but I have not verified specific bibliographic details. The claim that the original draft's "model-pool agnosticism" is architecturally false assumes a standard fixed-output-head design; if your scoring head is in fact pool-conditioned (e.g., takes model-card embeddings as input), the original claim could be salvageable and the revised paragraph should be adjusted.
